text: The video shows a complication from laparoscopy where the small intestine was injured by a trocar. Blood is visible on the mesentery and the intestine, indicating bleeding. A perforation in the small intestine is observed, with both entry and exit points from the trocar visible on opposite sides of the intestine.

The surgeon begins intracorporeal suturing to repair the trocar-induced injury on the small intestine. The entry wound is visibly wider than the exit wound, consistent with the pyramidal shape of the trocar. A 3-0 Vicryl suture is used to close the perforation.

These injuries are very common. One of the most common injury of the laparoscopy is the small bowel injury during access. In experienced hand these injuries are less but you never know even in the very experienced surgeon also this type of injury can happen. So because it is non-pathological traumatic injury so immediately if you will repair it then the outcome is very good and many a time the patient will not notice it also and it goes unnoticed. So cirrhosis has to be closed.

The surgeon performs seromuscular closure of the small intestine perforation using a surgeon's knot, ensuring that the opposite wall of the lumen is not included in the suture bite. Both the anterior and posterior walls are repaired to approximate the edges for healing.

So this patient post-operative outcome was very good and within twenty-four hour patient has bowel sound was found and then the patient has normal, actually the supraumbilical port was introduced in these patients and the trocar was very sharp and the surgeon has introduced it perpendicular actually

So this type of injury can be avoided if you will take care that the tip of the index finger should be pointed towards the tip of the chakar and slow screwing movement should be given. Most common cause of the injury of axis with the chakar is blunt and abrupt injury into the abdominal cavity because of either maybe a small incision size Or without screwing the trocar, if you will just try to puncture the abdominal wall, then suddenly as soon as giving way, suddenly stopping the trocar is sometimes difficult. If the index finger is pointed towards the sharp end, it will stop you.

The surgeon completes the repair of the anterior wall and proceeds to repair the posterior wall of the same layer using intracorporeal suturing. Through-and-through bites are taken to ensure complete approximation of the tissue edges. Fibrin glue is applied to seal the bowel injury, enhancing the repair.
The surgeon ties a surgeon's knot using intracorporeal suturing techniques, applying two sutures on the posterior wall and three bites on the anterior wall of the small bowel.
